The Gallery Reversal

For what it=E2=80=99s worth, Roth also presumably saw a lane to improve the=
 economic relationships between art and commerce. These days, it is not unc=
ommon for an artist to have multiple galleries selling their work across di=
fferent geographies. But the standard idea of a gallery as the representati=
ve and caretaker of the artist is now overblown. More and more, artists are=
 free agents. Yes, there are galleries that have been working with specific=
 artists for decades, and plenty of the older generation of artists will pe=
rpetuate that system. They provide the scholarly and archival infrastructur=
e that many artists need but cannot provide for themselves. Younger artists=
, however, are unlikely to stay with a single gallery for their careers.

That=E2=80=99s because these days, galleries have become distributors focus=
ed on the end user: They=E2=80=99re more
likely to cultivate a roster of collectors, either in their city or region =
or through a broader affinity, than artists. Larry Gagosian was a pioneer o=
f this gallery model. And we can see a very different strategy toward the s=
ame goal in Hauser & Wirth=E2=80=99s burgeoning hospitality empire.

$(ad3_title) ( https://pages.e.northerntrust.com/navigate-the-now-stress-te=
st.html?utm_medium=3Dnative&utm_source=3Dpuck&utm_campaign=3Dwmnational_grp=
-us-leadgen-families-20240915&utm_content=3Dwall_power_newsletter )

Many galleries now employ a so-called =E2=80=9Cartist=E2=80=99s liaison=E2=
=80=9D=E2=80=94but they work for the gallery and prioritize the gallery=E2=
=80=99s interests. Meanwhile, the demand for art has meant that artists=E2=
=80=99 studios have gotten larger. The studio manager, who used to just han=
dle the assistants and ordering supplies, has become tasked with determinin=
g which of the artist=E2=80=99s galleries get what work, and when. For mid-=
career and other well-established artists, the role has expanded further to=
 include managing museum shows and even handling press.

That=E2=80=99s a lot for someone with an MFA to deal with. Perhaps the only=
 reason the fine art manager hasn=E2=80=99t emerged is the lack of role mod=
els. There=E2=80=99s no obvious career path to trod and no ready supply of =
apprentice managers artists can easily hire. That said, there are some clea=
r examples of figures who add
value to the artists they work with. Cecily Brown has relied upon Andrea Cr=
ane, one of Teicher=E2=80=99s partners, for several years. Richard Prince=
=E2=80=99s business manager, Matt Gaughan, has been with him for a decade. =
Lawyer Joe Hage found a foothold in the art market working behind the scene=
s with Gerhard Richter. He=E2=80=99s since taken over managing Damien Hirst=
 and has a relationship with the Francis Bacon estate and with Peter Doig. =
The Ivorian artist Aboudia is said to have worked with a savvy representati=
ve when he was building his market a few years ago. There=E2=80=99s no way =
95-year-old Yayoi Kusama, with her many museum shows around the globe and g=
iant LVMH collab, doesn=E2=80=99t have a majordomo handling the details. Th=
ey just keep that person=E2=80=99s name out of the press. And there are oth=
ers, who act more as speculators, who have been taking positions in emergin=
g artists in recent years and trying to guide
their ascent.

Many of these relationships have formed in response to the fact that galler=
ies are now handling artists=E2=80=99 careers in a different way. During th=
e pandemic boom, galleries were pumping too much material into the market a=
nd raising prices too quickly, limiting the time span of an artist=E2=80=99=
s ability to be relevant. On the secondary market, few galleries defend the=
ir artists at auction=E2=80=94or even act as market makers for collectors w=
ho want to sell privately.

These are the market conditions that lead an artist to seek out a represent=
ative who can defend and advance their interests. It=E2=80=99s a story that=
 will sound familiar to anyone in L.A. who witnessed the rise of firms like=
 Endeavor and CAA. This may also be why UTA is not willing to throw in the =
towel entirely. Currently, it looks like the entertainment industry and the=
 art world are having trouble speaking the same language. Eventually, perha=
ps, all it will take is another unicorn like Josh
Roth, who can speak to both sides.

br/>Tonight, I=E2=80=99ve got a little news about UTA=E2=80=99s Fine Arts d=

re of the art market. UTA may be out of the art business, but the demand am=

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">As it turns out, Ch=
ristie=E2=80=99s C.E.O. <b>Guillaume Cerutti </b>wasn=E2=80=99t sugarcoatin=
g the prospects for the European market at a recent London art business con=
ference. According to the <i>FT</i>, he <u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.ne=
ws/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dSLUdXNWpnX0xreHZvNVpmeXBO=
dz0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvM2U3ZWJlMDMtZGQxNS00YTN=
hLTg4ZDgtMTc1YzE0ZWRjZWVkP3V0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1XYWxsK1Bvd2VyKy0rU1VCU0NSSUJFUl=
MrJTI4OSUyRjE1JTJGMjQlMjlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fY29udGVudD1XYWxsK1Bvd2VyKy0rU1VCU0NSS=
UJFUlMrJTI4OSUyRjElMkYyNCUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWxfYWN0aW9uXHUwMDI2=
dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1jdXN0b21lci5pb1x1MDAyNnV0bV90ZXJtPWY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZDEyYSI=
sImludGVybmFsIjoiZjZjNjA2MTdkYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwibGlua19pZCI6MTAzMzgwOX0/bc2fda=
c2cc76f80c4366e72dc778ae5a66f81d415e3b4458e7cc18e0723c7d7f" style=3D"color:=
#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf">estimated</a><=
/u> that =E2=80=9Cbetween 2010 and 2023, the value of sales at [Christie=E2=
=80=99s] grew 32 percent in the Americas and 22 percent in Asia Pacific, bu=
t fell 6 percent in Europe (including the U.K.). Across a similar period (2=
012-23), he reported that buyers from the Americas and Asia Pacific grew 27=
 percent and 33 percent respectively, while falling 37 percent in Europe.=
=E2=80=9D  <br/><br/>Naturally, that
context reduces the appeal of offering a work at auction in Europe this Oct=
ober. Nevertheless, Christie=E2=80=99s has secured one of <b>Jeff Koons</b>=
=E2=80=99 Balloon Monkey works from <b>Damien Hirst</b>=E2=80=99s collectio=
n to sell on October 9. The sculpture has an estimate of =C2=A36.5 million =
($8.5 million) and a guarantee. Two years ago, Christie=E2=80=99s sold <b>V=
ictor Pinchuk</b>=E2=80=99s magenta version of the same work for =C2=A310.1=
 million ($13.25 million). Pinchuk was, as many will remember, one of Hirst=
=E2=80=99s most important and visible collectors in the first decade of the=
 21st century. The orange version of the same sculpture sold in 2014, a mon=
th after the Whitney=E2=80=99s major retrospective for the artist closed, f=
or just shy of $26 million. That sale came a year after the record-setting =
sale of <i>Balloon Dog (Orange)</i>, which made $58 million, then a record =
for a living artist. There has been a fair bit of gossip in the industry ab=
out how that price was achieved, but <b>Mitchell</b> and
<b>Emily Rales</b> silenced some of that chatter when they paid <i>$91 mill=
ion </i>for the apex Koons inflatable, <i>Rabbit</i>, in 2019.<br/><br/>It=
=E2=80=99s easy for observers to get snarky over the decline in Koons=E2=80=
=99s prices at auction. But there are a number of important factors, especi=
ally for some of the inflatables. For one, Koons is a notorious perfectioni=
st. He had a bad habit of taking payments from collectors for these works a=
nd delaying delivery for years as the fabricators struggled to meet his par=
ameters. As a result, conflicts arose between collectors and the studio, wh=
ich could only ultimately depress demand.<br/><br/>There=E2=80=99s another =
overlooked dimension to these large colored balloon works. Koons created a =
large edition of plates with a miniature Balloon Dog mounted upon them as a=
 fundraiser for LACMA in 1995. The success of those editions has been repli=
cated with miniatures of the Balloon Monkey, Swan, and Rabbit. You can buy =
a Balloon Monkey in any of the
five colors from Bernardaud right now for $14,500. It=E2=80=99s an edition =
of 999 for each color. If you want to spend a little more for a miniature B=
alloon Dog, it will cost you $42,000.<br/><br/>Have these editioned miniatu=
res enhanced or suppressed the value of the originals? Years after that cli=
mactic retrospective, perhaps the ubiquity of these items has worked agains=
t the perceived value of the monumental works. Anyway, we=E2=80=99ll learn =
more about that in October. <br/><br/><i>And now, a few other notes from ac=
ross the art market universe=E2=80=A6</i><br/></td>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;"><ul style=3D"margin=
:0;color:#000000;font-size:16px;line-height:30px;margin-top:0;margin-bottom=
:0;list-style-type:square;mso-special-format:bullet"><li><i>Sotheby=E2=80=
=99s moves its Hong Kong sales</i>: Originally slated for the end of Septem=
ber to coincide with sales being held by Christie=E2=80=99s and Phillips, S=
otheby=E2=80=99s has decided to move their sales to November. =E2=80=9CThe =
groundbreaking new Maison in the heart of Hong Kong=E2=80=99s vibrant art s=
cene allows us to reinvent the sales calendar and format to meet clients wh=
ere they are,=E2=80=9D Sotheby=E2=80=99s Asia head <b>Nathan Drahi</b> emai=
led me. =E2=80=9CThe record-breaking traffic and notable new client acquisi=
tion we have experienced since opening in late July inspired us to rethink =
the timing for our sales to position them alongside innovative exhibitions,=
 which is our plan in November.=E2=80=9D The new dates for the Modern &
Contemporary Art day sale in Hong Kong are November 8 (for the exhibition) =
and November 13 (for the sale.)</li><br/><li><i>Christie=E2=80=99s buys a c=
lassic-car company</i>: The auction house is getting back into the vintage =
car business by acquiring Gooding & Co., which will allow president and=
 founder <b>David Gooding </b>to expand his business through Christie=E2=80=
=99s international platform and client network. It=E2=80=99s a homecoming o=
f sorts for Gooding, who had previously worked at Christie=E2=80=99s and RM=
 Auctions (another collector car auction company) before striking out on hi=
s own with his wife, <b>Dawn Ahrens</b>, in 2003. Since that time, Gooding =
& Co. has been the official auctioneer of the Pebble Beach Concours d=
=E2=80=99Elegance.<br/><br/>The deal also reflects a broader transformation=
 in the auction industry. In the old days, the houses made <i>headlines</i>=
 with seven-figure art sales but actually netted <i>profits</i> via the sal=
e of antiques, a catchall term for a wide range of
furniture and decorative objects. Over the last decade, the auction busines=
s remade itself so that the bottom of the pyramid is now dominated by the s=
ales of =E2=80=9Cluxury goods,=E2=80=9D a catchall term for everything from=
 jewelry, watches, and wine to vintage fashion, sneakers, and a range of ot=
her exotic collectibles. Christie=E2=80=99s sold more than $1 billion worth=
 of these luxury objects last year. So it is a serious business. <br/><br/>=
Classic cars don=E2=80=99t easily fit into one category or the other, and h=
ave flitted in and out of the industry as a result. Bonhams was previously =
owned by <b>Robert Brooks</b>, who rose through the car auction business at=
 Christie=E2=80=99s, and then with his own eponymous house, before he acqui=
red Bonhams and embarked upon an ambitious plan to rival Sotheby=E2=80=99s =
and Christie=E2=80=99s. (He eventually sold Bonhams to a private equity fir=
m.) Meanwhile, Sotheby=E2=80=99s slowly made its way into the classic car b=
usiness by acquiring a stake in RM before swallowing it whole
as RM Sotheby=E2=80=99s. If I had to guess, I would say Christie=E2=80=99s =
is getting back into the vintage car business for a couple of reasons. Firs=
t, they=E2=80=99re taking it to RM Sotheby=E2=80=99s in the forever war bet=
ween the two ancient auction houses. Second, the deal was announced as an a=
cquisition=E2=80=94with Gooding & Co. becoming Gooding Christie=E2=80=
=99s=E2=80=94but it=E2=80=99s unlikely to have cost much upfront, since Chr=
istie=E2=80=99s brings so many resources to expanding Gooding=E2=80=99s rea=
ch. Be that as it may, the future for anyone in the cultural property game =
is going to be figuring out which items become the focus of collectors'=
 =E2=80=9Cirrational=E2=80=9D interest in the future.</li> <br/><li><i>Korn=
feld results and the =E2=80=9Cart of the game=E2=80=9D</i>: Galerie Kornfel=
d, in Bern, sold works from the collection of its former owner and chief, <=
b>Eberhard Kornfeld</b>, this week. Ebi, as he was known, had a fabulous ey=
e, and the auction afforded many opportunities to acquire great works. <br/=
><br/>Some of those did very
well: A rare <b>Piet Mondrian</b> work on paper from his transitional phase=
 was priced at CHF 800,000=E2=80=94that=E2=80=99s Swiss francs, for the uni=
nitiated=E2=80=94but hammered for CHF <i>4.2 million</i> ($4.95 million). A=
 small <b>Giacometti</b> bust sold for CHF 3.8 million ($4.5 million) over =
a CHF 1 million estimate. A <b>Juan Gris</b> still life sold for CHF 3.4 mi=
llion ($4 million) over a CHF 1 million estimate. <b>Georges Seurat</b>=E2=
=80=99s <i>Dame au bouquets, de Dos</i>, made CHF 2.3 million ($2.7 million=
) above the CHF 2 million estimate. A <b>Paul C=C3=A9zanne </b>landscape so=
ld for CHF 2 million ($2.3 million), over a CHF 1.2 million estimate.  <b>C=
laude Monet</b>=E2=80=99s <i>Flood of the Seine at Vetheuil</i> sold for a =
CHF 2 million ($2.36 million) hammer price, exactly at the estimate. A prin=
t of <b>Edvard Munch</b>=E2=80=99s <i>Madonna</i> made CHF 1.5 million ($1.=
77 million), slightly more than double the estimate. A <b>Paul Klee</b> wat=
ercolor was hammered for CHF 1.15 million ($1.35
million) over a CHF 800k estimate.<br/><br/>In the separate sale devoted to=
 works by <b>Ernst Ludwig Kirchner</b>, the subject of Kornfeld=E2=80=99s s=
cholarly interest, most of the works that did sell went for the estimates. =
The one exception was a self-portrait estimated at CHF 640,000 ($750k) that=
 hammered for CHF 1.2 million ($1.4 million). I cannot tell you how success=
ful the sales were overall because Kornfeld has yet to post all the results=
, but let me leave you with the thoughts of an astute observer. =E2=80=9CDe=
spite these impressive results, a good number of lots failed to meet their =
reserve prices,=E2=80=9D art advisor <b>Libert=C3=A9 Nuti</b> wrote on Inst=
agram. =E2=80=9CThis underscores the importance of realistic estimates in t=
oday=E2=80=99s competitive market. Collections with prestigious provenance =
always draw attention and bidding, but low pricing is key to a successful s=
ale. That=E2=80=99s the art of the game!=E2=80=9D<br/></li></ul></td>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">UTA, the third-larg=
est Hollywood talent agency, has quietly closed its Fine Arts division, as =
well as the UTA Artist Spaces in Los Angeles and Atlanta. Some of the artis=
ts and art-adjacent figures represented by UTA recently got calls notifying=
 them that the agency was suspending their activities in the space. Divisio=
n director <b>Arthur Lewis</b>, a collector whose business background was m=
ostly in retail, at places like Old Navy, has decided to wrap things up. <b=
>Harrison Tenzer</b>, hired just a year ago, is no longer with the firm. <b=
r/><br/>Perhaps this ending was inevitable when <b>Jeremy Zimmer</b>, UTA=
=E2=80=99s chief executive, recruited <b>Joshua Roth</b> in 2015 to found t=
he division. Roth was a unicorn, uniquely placed by personal interest, fami=
ly pedigree, and training to figure out what it might mean for artists to h=
ave agents. An
entertainment lawyer with the high-powered firm of Glaser Weil, Roth was th=
e son of CAA co-founder<b> Steven Roth </b>and the scion of a family of art=
 collectors who once owned an oil company. Deeply embedded in Los Angeles=
=E2=80=99s art world=E2=80=94Roth=E2=80=99s wife <b>Sonya</b> runs Christie=
=E2=80=99s outpost in the city=E2=80=94he fused personal interest and socia=
l life into a new profession. <br/></td>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Even with that pedi=
gree and Zimmer=E2=80=99s support, it wasn=E2=80=99t clear that Roth had a =
straightforward business plan. He began by representing artists in ancillar=
y deals not connected to the primary pipeline of production and distributio=
n. But the art supply chain was changing rapidly. Eventually, it would open=
 a space for artists=E2=80=99 representatives. =E2=80=9CHaving a representa=
tive seems like an obvious transition into how artists want to be working i=
n the future,=E2=80=9D a London dealer told me the other week, before I hea=
rd about UTA shuttering its Fine Arts division. Unfortunately, Roth didn=E2=
=80=99t live to exploit that space. He died tragically in 2018 from heart f=
ailure, at age 40, while on a business trip to New York. <br/><br/>UTA=E2=
=80=99s exit from the art world=E2=80=94for now, at least=E2=80=94will come=
 as no surprise to anyone engaged in the buying and selling of art; it
was always hard to see how talent agents might fit into the equation. But t=
he retreat <i>is</i> surprising given the broader trends in the industry th=
at make the need for artists=E2=80=99 agents more pronounced, not less. UTA=
=E2=80=99s entry into the field came at a time when the agencies, fueled by=
 private equity money, were consolidating and UTA wanted to find new revenu=
e segments. It=E2=80=99s also true that art collecting has held something o=
f a special place in Los Angeles and the culture industry, where many try t=
o emulate <b>Jimmy Iovine</b>=E2=80=99s interest in art and some agents sti=
ll view <b>Michael Ovitz </b>as a spirit animal. <br/><br/><b>Max Teicher</=
b>, a former Gagosian director who now runs 291 Agency, recently made the p=
oint that the growth of galleries and auction houses into international beh=
emoths has already spawned the emergence of art advisors on one end of the =
supply chain. (Teicher says he=E2=80=99s seen an estimate that 40 percent o=
f art is bought through an advisor.) It
stands to reason that artists, on the other end of the supply chain, would =
need their own representatives. That should have been UTA=E2=80=99s opportu=
nity.<br/></td>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">For what it=E2=80=
=99s worth, Roth also presumably saw a lane to improve the economic relatio=
nships between art and commerce. These days, it is not uncommon for an arti=
st to have multiple galleries selling their work across different geographi=
es. But the standard idea of a gallery as the representative and caretaker =
of the artist is now overblown. More and more, artists are free agents. Yes=
, there are galleries that have been working with specific artists for deca=
des, and plenty of the older generation of artists will perpetuate that sys=
tem. They provide the scholarly and archival infrastructure that many artis=
ts need but cannot provide for themselves. Younger artists, however, are un=
likely to stay with a single gallery for their careers. <br/><br/>That=E2=
=80=99s because these days, galleries have become distributors focused on t=
he end user: They=E2=80=99re more
likely to cultivate a roster of <i>collectors</i>, either in their city or =
region or through a broader affinity, than artists. <b>Larry Gagosian</b> w=
as a pioneer of this gallery model. And we can see a very different strateg=
y toward the same goal in Hauser & Wirth=E2=80=99s burgeoning hospitali=
ty empire. <br/></td>
         </tr>

         <tr class=3D"replica" name=3D"s11">
          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Many galleries now =
employ a so-called =E2=80=9Cartist=E2=80=99s liaison=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94but t=
hey work for the gallery and prioritize the gallery=E2=80=99s interests. Me=
anwhile, the demand for art has meant that artists=E2=80=99 studios have go=
tten larger. The studio manager, who used to just handle the assistants and=
 ordering supplies, has become tasked with determining which of the artist=
=E2=80=99s galleries get what work, and when. For mid-career and other well=
-established artists, the role has expanded further to include managing mus=
eum shows and even handling press. <br/><br/>That=E2=80=99s a lot for someo=
ne with an MFA to deal with. Perhaps the only reason the fine art manager h=
asn=E2=80=99t emerged is the lack of role models. There=E2=80=99s no obviou=
s career path to trod and no ready supply of apprentice managers artists ca=
n easily hire. That said, there are some clear examples of figures who add
value to the artists they work with. <b>Cecily Brown </b>has relied upon <b=
>Andrea Crane</b>, one of Teicher=E2=80=99s partners, for several years. <b=
>Richard Prince</b>=E2=80=99s business manager, <b>Matt Gaughan</b>, has be=
en with him for a decade. Lawyer <b>Joe Hage </b>found a foothold in the ar=
t market working behind the scenes with <b>Gerhard Richter</b>. He=E2=80=99=
s since taken over managing <b>Damien Hirst </b>and has a relationship with=
 the <b>Francis Bacon</b> estate and with <b>Peter Doig</b>. The Ivorian ar=
tist <b>Aboudia</b> is said to have worked with a savvy representative when=
 he was building his market a few years ago. There=E2=80=99s no way 95-year=
-old <b>Yayoi Kusama</b>, with her many museum shows around the globe and g=
iant LVMH collab, doesn=E2=80=99t have a majordomo handling the details. Th=
ey just keep that person=E2=80=99s name out of the press. And there are oth=
ers, who act more as speculators, who have been taking positions in emergin=
g artists in recent years and trying to guide
their ascent. <br/><br/>Many of these relationships have formed in response=
 to the fact that galleries are now handling artists=E2=80=99 careers in a =
different way. During the pandemic boom, galleries were pumping too much ma=
terial into the market and raising prices too quickly, limiting the time sp=
an of an artist=E2=80=99s ability to be relevant. On the secondary market, =
few galleries defend their artists at auction=E2=80=94or even act as market=
 makers for collectors who want to sell privately. <br/><br/>These are the =
market conditions that lead an artist to seek out a representative who can =
defend and advance their interests. It=E2=80=99s a story that will sound fa=
miliar to anyone in L.A. who witnessed the rise of firms like Endeavor and =
CAA. This may also be why UTA is not willing to throw in the towel entirely=
=2E Currently, it looks like the entert=
ainment industry and the art world are=
 having trouble speaking the same language. Eventually, perhaps, all it wil=
l take is another unicorn like Josh
Roth, who can speak to both sides. <br/></td>
